sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Hydrofluoric acid (HF) is uniquely dangerous among acids. Unlike other acids whose damage is caused by hydrogen ions (H+), HF causes injury through the fluoride ion (F-), which penetrates tissue, binds calcium and magnesium, and causes deep necrosis and potentially fatal systemic toxicity.

**Small surface area exposures can be fatal.** Exposures >1% TBSA with concentrated HF (>50%) have caused death from cardiac arrhythmia.

**Mine Site Uses**
- Mineral processing (uranium, phosphate, rare earths)
- Metal surface treatment and rust removal
- Laboratory reagents
- Rust converter products (dilute HF)

**Concentration and Injury Pattern**
| HF Concentration | Pain Onset | Injury Depth |
|---|---|---|
| <20% (dilute) | Delayed 2–8 hours | May appear minor; deep necrosis can follow |
| 20–50% | 1–8 hours | Significant tissue damage |
| >50% (concentrated) | Immediate, severe | Immediate necrosis; high systemic risk |

**Systemic Toxicity Mechanism**
Fluoride ions:
1. Bind calcium → **hypocalcaemia** → tetany, prolonged QT, VF
2. Bind magnesium → **hypomagnesaemia**
3. Inhibit Na+/K+-ATPase → **hyperkalaemia** → VF
4. Direct cellular metabolic inhibition

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Local (Skin/Eye)**
- Skin: erythema, blistering, white or grey-white necrosis at exposure site
- **Severe, burning, deep pain** — often disproportionate to visible skin change
- With dilute HF: pain onset may be delayed 2–8 hours; area may appear normal initially
- Eyes: severe burning pain, conjunctival injection, lacrimation, photophobia; corneal damage if delayed treatment

**Inhalation Injury**
- Severe throat and chest burning
- Stridor, bronchospasm, dyspnoea
- Haemoptysis
- Pulmonary oedema (potentially delayed)

**Systemic Toxicity (Life-Threatening)**
- Paraesthesia, perioral tingling (hypocalcaemia)
- Muscle cramps, tetany, Chvostek's/Trousseau's signs
- Cardiac arrhythmias — QT prolongation, VF — may occur with **no warning**
- Hypotension, cardiovascular collapse
- Seizures

**Red Flags — Escalate Immediately**
- Any inhalation exposure
- Face or eye exposure
- Exposure >1% TBSA (palm = ~1%)
- Concentrated HF (>20%) any exposure
- Tetany, perioral tingling, arrhythmia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**Calcium Gluconate Gel — First-Line Topical Treatment**
- **Calcium gluconate 2.5% gel** is the specific antidote — binds fluoride ion in tissue, halting necrosis
- Available as Calgonate gel or can be made from 10% IV solution
- Apply immediately and massage continuously through gloves — pain relief indicates adequate fluoride binding
- Continue application every 15 minutes until pain resolves and does not recur
- Do **NOT** use calcium chloride topically — causes severe tissue necrosis

**Pain as Treatment Guide**
- Persistent pain after irrigation and calcium gluconate application indicates ongoing fluoride activity
- Complete pain relief signals adequate fluoride binding
- Recurrence of pain indicates further fluoride activity — repeat application

**Do NOT Underestimate**
- A small apparently minor blister from dilute HF can be life-threatening if not treated
- Delayed pain with dilute HF does not mean the exposure was minor

**Diphoterine**
- If Diphoterine eyewash or skin wash is available, use it for initial decontamination
- Follow with calcium gluconate gel for skin exposures

**Systemic Treatment**
- IV calcium gluconate is required if systemic signs develop OR for significant exposures (>1% TBSA, concentrated HF)
- Do NOT delay — cardiac arrhythmia can develop without warning

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
**Immediate Decontamination**
1. **Remove clothing, gloves, shoes** contaminated with HF immediately
2. **Copious water irrigation** for minimum 15 minutes (or Diphoterine if available)
3. **Apply calcium gluconate 2.5% gel** to skin exposure and massage gently through gloves
4. **Eye exposure:** copious water or Diphoterine irrigation for 15 minutes, then calcium gluconate 1% eye drops if available; URGENT ophthalmology referral

**Ongoing Management**
- Continue calcium gluconate gel massage until pain resolves (may need repeated application)
- Establish IV access — all significant HF exposures require IV access
- **12-lead ECG** — QT interval monitoring mandatory
- Continuous cardiac monitoring
- Call RFDS — all HF burns require hospital evaluation

**Systemic Analgesia**
- Morphine IV titrated to pain relief
- Avoid NSAIDs (renal effects)

**Inhalation Exposure**
- High-flow oxygen 15 L/min NRB
- Salbutamol nebulised for bronchospasm
- Urgent RFDS — inhalation HF can cause rapid fatal pulmonary oedema

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
**IV Calcium Gluconate (Systemic HF Toxicity or Significant Exposure)**
**Indications:**
- Any systemic signs (tetany, arrhythmia, QT >500ms, tetany, carpopedal spasm)
- Concentrated HF exposure >1% TBSA
- Inhalation exposure
- Digit/hand exposure with pain not controlled by topical gel

**Dose:**
- Calcium gluconate 10%: 0.1–0.2 mL/kg (up to 10 mL in adults) IV over 10 minutes
- Can repeat as required with ECG and clinical monitoring
- Aim for normalisation of QT interval and resolution of symptoms
- Infusion: calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL in 50 mL normal saline over 2–4 hours

**Cardiac Arrhythmia**
- VF from HF: defibrillate + IV calcium gluconate (not just standard ACLS)
- QT prolongation: calcium gluconate IV; avoid drugs that prolong QT
- Continuous ECG monitoring throughout

**Finger/Digit Exposure — Arterial Calcium Gluconate**
- Hospital-based procedure: intra-arterial calcium gluconate infusion for digit exposures not responding to IV/topical treatment
- Alert receiving hospital that digit HF exposure has occurred

**Airway**
- Inhalation HF may cause rapid airway oedema
- Early intubation if stridor or airway compromise

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Emergency Contacts**
- **Poisons Information Centre: 13 11 26** — available 24/7 for guidance
- **RFDS Perth 24/7: 1800 625 800**

**All HF Burns Require Hospital Assessment**
Even if the burn appears minor, all HF exposures require:
- Hospital assessment and CK, calcium, potassium, ECG monitoring
- Minimum 6–12 hours observation for systemic toxicity
- Surgical assessment for possible debridement and intralesional calcium treatment

**Site Preparedness**
- All sites using HF should have calcium gluconate 2.5% gel immediately available at point of use (not just the clinic)
- Eyewash stations and emergency shower within 10 seconds of any HF use
- All HF workers trained in recognition and first aid
- Antidote gel must be within reach in the work area — seconds matter
